meta.title: C208 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes CLK C208 engine — M111, M112, M113. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the supercharged four-cylinder explained. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes CLK C208 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned C208 engine typically costs £1,200–£9,000 supply only, depending on engine — the M111 four-cylinders sit at £1,200–£3,200, the M112 V6 at £2,200–£4,500, and the M113 V8 at £4,000–£9,000. Fitting adds £520–£1,300 depending on engine complexity.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes CLK C208 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M112.940 V6 in the CLK 320 is our top recommendation — it's a proven, naturally aspirated six-cylinder with a documented reputation for exceeding 150,000 miles with routine maintenance. The M112.912 in the CLK 240 shares the same robust design with slightly less power and better economy.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes CLK C208?

acceptedAnswer.text: Supercharger clutch and timing chain wear on the M111 Kompressor engines (CLK 200/230), camshaft adjuster wear on the M112 V6 (CLK 240/320), valve cover oil leaks on the M113 V8 (CLK 430/55 AMG), and — unrelated to the engine — conductor plate failure on the 722.6 five-speed automatic transmission.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes CLK C208 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, if you choose carefully. The M112 V6 is the generation's most dependable engine — simple, robust, and well-understood. The M113 V8 is characterful and powerful, but oil leaks are a cost-of-ownership factor. The M111 four-cylinder is reliable but the supercharger adds complexity. On any C208, the 722.6 transmission's conductor plate is the single most common non-engine failure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes CLK C208 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is an outright 'avoid', but a supercharged M111 (CLK 200/230 Kompressor) without documented supercharger service history is the one purchase we'd counsel caution on — clutch and tensioner repairs can quickly exceed the car's value on budget examples. A neglected CLK 430/55 with V8 oil leaks is the second.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my C208 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Supercharger whine or loss of boost (M111 Kompressor), cold-start rattle or rough idle (M112 V6 camshaft adjusters), or persistent oil leaks and burning smell (M113 V8) are the key warning signs. Check engine lights on any variant should be diagnosed immediately.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes CLK C208 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £6,000 CLK 320, a £3,000–£4,500 M112 recon is 50–75% — borderline. Against a tired £3,000 example, the same recon exceeds value — walk away. For the V8, against a £8,000+ CLK 430/55, a £5,000–£9,000 recon is 50–100% — viable only on exceptional examples. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes CLK C208 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M112 V6 can exceed 200,000 miles with diligent maintenance — camshaft adjuster wear is the limiting factor. The M113 V8 is similarly robust, with oil leaks the main cost, not bottom-end failure. The M111 four-cylinder can reach 150,000+ miles, but the supercharger clutch and timing chain tensioner will likely need attention before then.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the C208 and the A208?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are first-generation CLK models sharing the same platform and engines. The C208 is the 2-door coupé (1997–2002, this page), while the A208 is the 2-door cabriolet (1998–2003). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see the A208 generation page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes CLK C208 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate under the bonnet and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Tell us your variant (e.g., CLK 320) and approximate registration year (pre- or post-2000 facelift) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes CLK C208 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first-generation CLK coupé — from the entry-level M111 four-cylinder to the thundering M113 V8 in the CLK 55 AMG. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the supercharged four-cylinder that defined this generation's personality. Backed by 890+ real UK M112 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes CLK C208 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C208 coupé specifically — including the 1999 facelift and 2000 engine refresh that reshaped the range.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: M112: 90% confidence — 890 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries in 2025; failure patterns well-documented. M113: 85% confidence — well-understood V8 with documented failure patterns. M111: 70% confidence — pattern-data tier; no separate EM enquiry figure exists.

overview.intro: The C208 is the coupé variant of Mercedes-Benz's first-generation CLK-Class, introduced in 1997 as the successor to the C124 E-Class coupé. Based on the W202 C-Class platform, it combined E-Class styling cues with C-Class underpinnings. A 1999 facelift brought cosmetic updates, while a 2000 engine refresh reshaped the four-cylinder range. The range-topping CLK 55 AMG, introduced in 1999, delivered 342 bhp from a hand-assembled 5.4-litre V8.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz W202 (C-Class) • Predecessor: Mercedes-Benz E-Class C124 • Successor: Mercedes-Benz CLK C209 • Sibling body styles in this generation: A208 cabriolet (1998–2003).
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The M112 V6 engine family generated 890 real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], concentrated on the CLK 320 as these ageing coupés enter classic-car territory.

items.quote: no diesel engine fitted to the C208.

The 3.2-litre V6 offers the perfect balance of refinement and reliability — smooth power delivery, no supercharger complexity, and a documented reputation for exceeding 150,000 miles with routine maintenance. Camshaft adjuster wear is its only real demand.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 smoothness without V8 running costs.

items.quote: The supercharged 2.0-litre four-cylinder is a clever piece of engineering, but the supercharger clutch and timing chain tensioner are known weak points — and the 2000 refresh detuned it for a reason.
items.whoItsFor: only buyers with documented supercharger service history.

items.quote: The 5.4-litre V8 delivers 342 bhp and a soundtrack to match, but it's an ageing AMG — valve cover oil leaks, timing chain wear, and the sheer cost of V8 parts make it the most expensive engine in the range to maintain.
items.whoItsFor: AMG collectors with deep pockets and full service history.

items.quote: The V6 offers genuine performance (7.4s to 60mph) with significantly lower running costs than the V8 — and sits in the same used price bracket as the four-cylinder cars. It's the sweet spot of the range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best all-round CLK experience.

items.quote: Often overshadowed by the 320, the 2.6-litre V6 offers the same smooth six-cylinder character with slightly better fuel economy — and lower purchase prices because buyers overlook it.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 refinement on a budget.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Supercharged Risk — budget for charger work.
More Power, Same Risk — 230 shares 200's issues.
Best V6 — smooth, dependable, great value.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve cover oil leaks, timing chain wear
Valve cover oil leaks, timing chain
rows.ownershipVerdict: AMG Icon — high power, high cost.

economicsRule.title: The C208 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£4,500 M112 recon against a £3,000–£8,000 C208 sits at 30–150% of the car's value — on sub-£4,000 examples, it brushes or exceeds our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. The M113 V8 recon at £4,000–£9,000 against a £5,000–£12,000 CLK 430/55 is 30–80% — viable on good examples, but walk away from tired ones. Value the car first; the engine is often worth more than the car on budget examples.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C208 Costs Least To Own?
Which C208 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: CLK 55 AMG — V8 parts and specialist labour costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C208 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: CLK 320 (M112.940) — V6 smoothness without V8 running costs.

problems.title: M111 Supercharger Clutch & Timing Chain Wear
problems.affectedModels: C208 CLK 200 Kompressor, CLK 230 Kompressor (all M111 supercharged variants).
problems.rootCause: The supercharger clutch engages and disengages under load — wear leads to clutch failure, loss of boost, and in some cases, supercharger seizure. The timing chain tensioner is also a known weak point on higher-mileage M111 engines.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of supercharger whine or loss of boost, have the clutch and belt inspected immediately — a £400–£1,200 specialist repair is far cheaper than the £2,500+ replacement that follows if the supercharger fails catastrophically.

cta.label: Compare M111 engine prices for your C208 CLK 200 Kompressor →

problems.title: M112 V6 Camshaft Adjuster Wear
problems.affectedModels: C208 CLK 240, CLK 320 (all M112.912 and M112.940 variants).
problems.rootCause: The camshaft adjusters (variable valve timing) wear over time, leading to rattling on start-up, reduced power, and eventually check engine lights. Oil leaks from the camshaft covers are a secondary but common issue.
problems.recommendation: If your CLK 240 or 320 rattles on cold start, don't delay — a £500–£1,000 adjuster replacement is a fraction of the £3,500+ engine replacement that follows if the adjuster fails entirely.

cta.label: Compare M112 engine prices for your C208 CLK 320 →

problems.title: M113 V8 Valve Cover Oil Leaks
problems.affectedModels: C208 CLK 430, CLK 55 AMG (all M113.943 and M113.984 variants).
problems.rootCause: The V8's valve cover gaskets degrade with age and heat cycling — oil leaks are the most common complaint, with oil seeping onto exhaust manifolds and causing burning smells.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of oil smell or visible seepage, have the gaskets replaced — a £600–£1,500 specialist repair is far cheaper than the £5,000+ replacement that follows if oil loss leads to engine damage.

cta.label: Compare M113 engine prices for your C208 CLK 430 →

problems.title: 5G-Tronic Transmission (722.6) Conductor Plate Failure
problems.affectedModels: All C208 variants with automatic transmission.
problems.rootCause: The 722.6 five-speed automatic's conductor plate (which houses the speed sensors and valve body electronics) fails with age — leading to gear selection issues, limp mode, and eventually transmission failure.
problems.recommendation: On any C208 over 80,000 miles, treat the gearbox service as non-negotiable — a £500 conductor plate replacement is cheap insurance against a £2,500+ transmission replacement.

replacementCosts.h2: C208 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the baseline figures — the C208 coupé shares its W202 platform with the C-Class of the era, and engine-bay access is consistent across the range.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are C208 coupé only (1997–2002). A208 cabriolet (1998–2003) shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, since fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style. Note that the CLK 200 Kompressor was detuned from 192 PS to 163 PS in the 2000 engine refresh.
